informal: Suppose $p \in \mathcal{P}(\mathbf{C})$ has degree $m$. Prove that $p$ has $m$ distinct roots if and only if $p$ and its derivative $p^{\prime}$ have no roots in common.
reasoning: The hypothesis `hm : m = p.degree` (with `m : ℕ` coerced into `WithBot ℕ`) together with `hp : p ≠ 0` correctly captures "p has degree m". The left side `card (p.rootSet ℂ) = m` says the set of complex roots of p has exactly m elements, i.e. p has m distinct roots (since p has at most m roots, this is equivalent to "m distinct roots"). The right side `Disjoint (p.rootSet ℂ) ((derivative p).rootSet ℂ)` says p and p' share no complex root. The edge case m = 0 still works: p is a nonzero constant with empty rootSet, p' = 0 whose rootSet is also empty, so disjointness holds and card = 0. The encoding matches the informal statement.
